edges.description: Mutant SLC19A3 limits the adaptive thiamine-transport response needed during physiologic stress.
Cerebral thiamine-transport failure produces Wernicke-like injury with basal ganglia vulnerability.
Severe infantile presentations may include elevated lactate/lactic acidosis during metabolic decompensation.
intermediate_mechanisms: metabolic decompensation during infantile encephalopathic presentations

edges.description: Cerebral gray-matter injury manifests clinically as acute or subacute encephalopathy.
The encephalopathic gray-matter injury state includes confusion or altered mental status.
Adult Wernicke-like BTBGD gray-matter injury can present with rapidly progressive cognitive impairment.
The acute encephalopathic CNS injury state can include hypersomnolence during BTBGD episodes.
Injured cerebral networks during BTBGD episodes are associated with seizures.
Basal ganglia injury produces extrapyramidal movement disorder and dystonia.
Basal ganglia dysfunction in classic BTBGD produces extrapyramidal cogwheel rigidity.
BTBGD can include corticospinal or upper-motor-neuron signs such as hyperreflexia during classic presentations.
Corticospinal involvement in classic BTBGD can manifest as ankle clonus.
Corticospinal involvement in classic BTBGD can manifest as Babinski responses.
BTBGD encephalopathic CNS injury can include bulbar dysfunction with dysphagia.
BTBGD encephalopathic CNS injury can include dysarthria.
Wernicke-like gray-matter involvement can present with ataxia in adult BTBGD.
Basal ganglia lesions are visible as characteristic bilateral MRI signal abnormalities.

edges.source: Avoidance of sodium valproate and ACTH
Avoidance of sodium valproate and ACTH

edges.description: Elevated blood lactate is the biochemical measurement underlying the lactic acidosis phenotype in infantile BTBGD presentations.

nodes.description: Ankle clonus is a common upper-motor-neuron sign in classic BTBGD.

evidence.snippet: hyperreflexia, ankle clonus, and Babinski responses are common.

nodes.description: Ataxia has been reported in adult Wernicke-like BTBGD presentations.

evidence.snippet: We report a 49-year-old lady presenting with rapidly progressive cognitive impairment, seizures, hypersomnolence, ataxia, and generalized dystonia of 3 weeks duration.

nodes.description: Sodium valproate should be avoided for epilepsy treatment in BTBGD, and ACTH used for epileptic spasms can induce status dystonicus.
nodes.id: Avoidance of sodium valproate and ACTH

evidence.snippet: Avoid use of sodium valproate to treat epilepsy. Use of ACTH to treat epileptic spasms can induce status dystonicus.

nodes.description: Extensor plantar responses (Babinski sign) are a common corticospinal sign in classic BTBGD.

evidence.snippet: hyperreflexia, ankle clonus, and Babinski responses are common.

nodes.description: Impaired cerebral thiamine transport injures basal ganglia and other gray matter regions, producing bilateral basal ganglia lesions, Wernicke-like pathology, seizures, encephalopathy, and movement disorder.

evidence.snippet: These results demonstrate ubiquitously reduced thiamine transporter function in the cerebral gray matter, and neuropathological alterations similar to Wernicke's disease in BTBGD.
The central nervous system lesions found in the patients displayed almost clearly a specific pattern for SLC19A3 defect, as previously proposed in diagnostic criteria.

meta.association: Biallelic SLC19A3 pathogenic variants cause BTBGD.

evidence.snippet: SLC19A3 | solute carrier family 19 member 3 | hgnc:16266 | Disease-causing germline mutation(s) in
Whole exome sequencing (WES) was performed at Baylor College of Medicine, Medical Genetics Laboratory and confirmed compound heterozygous frameshift mutations in the SLC19A3 gene in trans

nodes.description: Brain MRI commonly shows bilateral signal abnormalities or lesions in the caudate nucleus, putamen, globus pallidus, and related basal ganglia structures, and may also show thalamic, cortical, subcortical white matter, brainstem, cerebellar, or cervical-spine involvement.

evidence.snippet: Imaging may reveal bilateral lesions in the basal ganglia.
Children presenting with unexplained encephalopathy and MRI abnormalities including bilateral signal alteration of caudate nucleus and putamen should raise the suspicion for BTBGD
The magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the brain revealed T2-hyperintensities in the basal ganglia, thalamus, cortical, subcortical regions with striatal necrosis suggestive of BTBGD

Typical MRI findings include cortical, subcortical white matter lesions, and bilateral abnormal signal intensity in the basal ganglia with less frequent involvement of thalami, brain stem, cerebellum and cervical spine
Four different phenotypes have been described; infantile lactic acidosis with encephalopathy
Biochemical analysis revealed elevated blood lactate at 3.8 mmol/L (nl 0.6-2.2)

readouts.interpretation: Elevated blood lactate is the biochemical measurement underlying the lactic acidosis phenotype in infantile BTBGD presentations.

nodes.description: Rapidly progressive cognitive impairment can be a prominent feature, particularly in adult Wernicke-like BTBGD presentations.

evidence.reference_title: Biotin-Responsive Basal Ganglia Disease: Treatable Metabolic Disorder with SLC19A3 Mutation Presenting as Rapidly Progressive Dementia.
evidence.snippet: We report a 49-year-old lady presenting with rapidly progressive cognitive impairment, seizures, hypersomnolence, ataxia, and generalized dystonia of 3 weeks duration.

nodes.description: Cogwheel rigidity is nearly always present in classic BTBGD, consistent with extrapyramidal basal ganglia dysfunction.

evidence.snippet: Dystonia and cogwheel rigidity are nearly always present

nodes.description: Confusion accompanies the encephalopathic episodes.

evidence.snippet: A rare genetic neurological disorder characterized by subacute encephalopathy with confusion, seizures, and movement disorder, often following a history of febrile illness.

nodes.description: Dysarthria can occur during BTBGD encephalopathic presentations.

evidence.snippet: The disease is characterized by subacute encephalopathy with confusion, dysphagia, dysarthria, and seizures.

nodes.description: Dysphagia can occur during BTBGD encephalopathic presentations.

evidence.snippet: The disease is characterized by subacute encephalopathy with confusion, dysphagia, dysarthria, and seizures.

nodes.description: Immediate high-dose thiamine and biotin supplementation is the core disease-specific therapy and should be started when BTBGD is suspected.

evidence.snippet: The disease usually becomes symptomatic in childhood and is life-threatening if left untreated, but symptoms can be reversed and progression prevented by treatment with high doses of biotin and thiamine.
High doses of biotin and thiamine are the mainstay of the treatment to achieve a favorable outcome.
It is considered a treatable condition if vitamin supplementation, most commonly with thiamine and biotin, is initiated early.

nodes.description: Hyperreflexia is a common neurologic sign in classic BTBGD.

evidence.snippet: hyperreflexia, ankle clonus, and Babinski responses are common.

nodes.description: Excessive sleepiness can accompany the acute encephalopathic presentation of BTBGD.

evidence.reference_title: Biotin-Responsive Basal Ganglia Disease: Treatable Metabolic Disorder with SLC19A3 Mutation Presenting as Rapidly Progressive Dementia.
evidence.snippet: We report a 49-year-old lady presenting with rapidly progressive cognitive impairment, seizures, hypersomnolence, ataxia, and generalized dystonia of 3 weeks duration.

nodes.description: Infantile BTBGD presentations may include lactic acidosis or elevated blood lactate during encephalopathic decompensation.

evidence.snippet: Four different phenotypes have been described; infantile lactic acidosis with encephalopathy
Biochemical analysis revealed elevated blood lactate at 3.8 mmol/L (nl 0.6-2.2)

nodes.description: Movement disorder is part of the defining phenotype and may include generalized dystonia.

evidence.snippet: A rare genetic neurological disorder characterized by subacute encephalopathy with confusion, seizures, and movement disorder, often following a history of febrile illness.
We report a 49-year-old lady presenting with rapidly progressive cognitive impairment, seizures, hypersomnolence, ataxia, and generalized dystonia of 3 weeks duration.

nodes.description: Pathogenic SLC19A3 variants reduce thiamine transporter 2 function, limiting thiamine transport into and within the central nervous system.

evidence.snippet: Biotin-thiamine-responsive basal ganglia disease (BTBGD) is a potentially treatable disorder caused by mutations in the SLC19A3 gene, encoding the human thiamine transporter 2.
Biotin thiamine responsive basal ganglia disease (BTRBGD) is an inherited autosomal recessive disorder that results from the inability of thiamine to cross the blood-brain barrier.

nodes.description: Seizures are a common component of acute BTBGD episodes.

evidence.snippet: A rare genetic neurological disorder characterized by subacute encephalopathy with confusion, seizures, and movement disorder, often following a history of febrile illness.
We report a 49-year-old lady presenting with rapidly progressive cognitive impairment, seizures, hypersomnolence, ataxia, and generalized dystonia of 3 weeks duration.

nodes.description: Febrile illness, acidosis, hypoxia, and other stressors fail to induce adequate SLC19A3 upregulation in patient cells, reducing adaptive thiamine availability when neuronal demand is high.

evidence.snippet: exposure of fibroblasts to stress factors such as acidosis or hypoxia markedly upregulated SLC19A3 in control cells, but failed to elevate SLC19A3 expression in the patient's fibroblasts.
They also suggest that episodes of encephalopathy are caused by a substantially reduced capacity of mutant neuronal cells to increase SLC19A3 expression, necessary to adapt to stress conditions.

nodes.description: Acute or subacute encephalopathy with confusion or altered mental status is the core clinical presentation.

evidence.snippet: A rare genetic neurological disorder characterized by subacute encephalopathy with confusion, seizures, and movement disorder, often following a history of febrile illness.
The disease is characterized by subacute encephalopathy with confusion, dysphagia, dysarthria, and seizures.
